name: VADER1
description: Red-emitting voltage indicator built on the JEDI/ASAP architecture with a circularly permuted mApple for reliable spike detection under in vivo 2P.

spectrum.name: VADER1
spectrum.note: VADER1 is not in FPbase; the displayed curve is the parent fluorophore mApple (VADER1's reporter is a circularly permuted mApple, cpmApple — the same fluorophore as the FlicR series). The stored excitation/emission arrays are the FPbase mApple spectrum verbatim (correlation 1.0000, all points identical; ex 567 / em 592 nm). The paper reports VADER1's own measured 1P excitation and emission spectra (Fig. S2.1; Fig. 2B right panel) and states they "closely matched mApple's, with a modest blue shift," so the mApple curve is a faithful proxy for VADER1's peaks.

kinetics.note: Activation τ = 1.41 ms (monoexponential) for a 100-mV depolarization step (−70→+30 mV). Repolarization (+30→−70 mV) is biexponential: τfast = 0.79 ms (77 ± 5%), τslow = 20 ± 8 ms (23%) → amplitude-weighted mean τ̄off = 0.77×0.79 + 0.23×20 = 5.21 ms (stored; the fast 0.79-ms component alone understates the response). Whole-cell voltage clamp, 1P widefield, HEK293A cells (Table 1, n = 13). The dominant fast component keeps spike repolarization well-resolved despite the slow tail.

dynamicRangeData.note: 1P steady-state ΔF/F from -70 to +30 mV (100-mV step), measured by whole-cell voltage clamp combined with 1P widefield imaging at ~22°C (median ~42%, range ~22-58%, n = 5-11 HEK293A cells).

2P steady-state ΔF/F under whole-cell voltage clamp with 2P illumination at 1050 nm (~22°C, n = 13 cells). 130 ± 20% (mean ± 95% CI) is the paper's headline 2P dynamic range, quantified in Fig. 2E as the −40→+30 mV (70-mV) suprathreshold step. Read off the Fig. 2D F-V curve (referenced to −70 mV), ΔF/F at +30 mV (the −70→+30 100-mV step) is ~130-140%, since the subthreshold −70→−40 segment contributes only ~5-10%; 130 is stored. The full −120 to +90 mV range (210-mV span) gives 192 ± 25%. The 2P ΔF/F greatly exceeds the 1P value (~42%) for the comparable window — a real modality difference the paper reports.

sensitivityData.note: Peak ΔF/F response to an isolated single-action-potential waveform replayed under voltage clamp, imaged by 2P resonant scanning at 440 Hz in vitro (n = 10 cells).

Mean in vivo single-spike response amplitude of VADER1-tKv in Layer 2/3 cortical neurons of awake, head-fixed mice under ULoVE 2P microscopy at 1045 nm (68.9 ± 16.5% ΔF/F, mean ± SD, n = 20 cells); ~2.5-fold larger than JEDI-2P-Kv.

apWidthData.sample: Mouse, visual cortex L2/3, awake head-fixed, in vivo
apWidthData.note: Optical action-potential waveform width (full width at half maximum) of VADER1-tKv single spikes recorded in vivo under ULoVE 2P microscopy: 1.2 ± 0.2 ms (mean ± SD), statistically indistinguishable from JEDI-2P-Kv (1.4 ± 0.3 ms). Sampling rate 7.2 kHz (7142.9 Hz, two ULoVE volumes/cell: 2 × (50 µs acquisition + 20 µs AOD access)); awake head-fixed mice, body temperature ~37°C. Spike width was taken as the FWHM of the spike-triggered average waveform after 20 kHz linear interpolation.

apWidthData.sample: HEK293a cells, whole-cell voltage clamp, in vitro
apWidthData.note: Optical FWHM (~6 ms) of VADER1's fluorescence response to an isolated AP waveform (100-mV amplitude, 4-ms-FWHM electrical spike) imaged under 2P resonant scanning at 440 Hz (1050 nm, ~45 mW), whole-cell voltage clamp of stable HEK293a lines at room temperature (~25°C). The ~6-ms optical width vs ~4-ms electrical spike reflects modest broadening from indicator kinetics and the 440-Hz frame rate undersampling the response (main text). Value read from the Fig. S2.2A scatter (Y-axis 0-8 ms, points clustered near 6 ms).

brightnessData.note: 2P basal brightness at -70 mV, corrected for relative 2P excitation efficiency at 1050 nm. Relative brightness normalized to VADER1 = 1.0; FlicR2 = 1.18 (n = 24 wells, >100 HEK-Kir2.1 cells each), so VADER1/FlicR2 = 0.85.

photostabilityData.note: In vivo 2P ULoVE photobleaching: baseline fluorescence remained at ~60% of its initial level after 10 min of continuous illumination at 22.5 mW measured at the brain surface (1045 nm). Bleaching was biexponential with a fast component comparable to JEDI-2P; slow-phase power-law slope -0.135 (vs JEDI-2P -0.056). NOTE: power-only (not an mW/mm² irradiance) and 2P, so not directly comparable to the 1-min/100-mW-per-mm² 1P standard.

photobleach.note: Model-free photostability metric: t75% = time for fluorescence to fall to 75% of its initial value. Digitized from the VADER1 (red) slow-phase bleach curve in Fig. 3F (in vivo ULoVE 2P, 1045 nm, 22.5 mW at the brain surface). The slow phase follows a power law F(t)=a·t^b (t in s); fitting the digitized points gives b=-0.124 (the paper reports -0.135 for the same curve; R²=0.98), F(60s)=0.80 and F(600s)=0.60 (consistent with the ~60%-remaining-at-10-min figure). Solving F(t)=0.75 gives t75% ≈ 104 s ≈ 1.7 min. The preceding rapid phase (Fig. 3E) drops F from 1.0 to ~0.80 within the first minute but never reaches 0.75, so the 75% crossing lies in the slow-phase regime shown here. This is a 2P, power-only (not mW/mm²) measurement, so it is NOT intensity-normalized and is not directly comparable to 1P GEVIs.

twoPhoton.note: First fully genetically encoded red GEVI demonstrating robust 2P voltage imaging in vivo. Reliable single-spike detection under both ULoVE (~1045 nm) and resonant-scanning 2P microscopy in mouse cortex L2/3 and L5; 1040-1130 nm excitation exceeds 80% of maximal brightness, with peaks at 1055 and 1120 nm.

researchPapers.title: A red-emitting, genetically encoded indicator for two-photon voltage recording in vivo
researchPapers.authors: Yang S, McDonald AJ, Lu X, Villette V, Hakam N, Galdamez M, et al.
researchPapers.sample: HEK293 cells, in vitro; Rat, cultured hippocampal neurons, in vitro; Mouse, visual cortex L2/3 and L5, in vivo
